skill.name: image-to-video
skill.description: Guide for converting still images into short AI videos with inference.sh models, motion prompts, camera movement, and workflow recommendations.
skill.summary: Plan and prompt image-to-video generation workflows using inference.sh models.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Ruby backtick execution and blocker patterns, but review shows most are markdown code fences, inline code, model IDs, or descriptive text rather than executable Ruby. One confirmed risk remains: the quick start recommends piping a remote installer into a shell, and the skill also encourages networked CLI execution against external inference services.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The quick start tells users to run a remote install script through sh. This is a real supply-chain risk because users execute whatever the remote endpoint returns at install time.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command directly pipes a URL response into sh. The surrounding note describes checksum verification, but the shell-pipe installation pattern remains risky.

medium_findings.title: Networked External CLI Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands and includes many examples that send prompts and local media paths to external inference apps. This is expected for the skill, but users should understand the privacy and cost implications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and command examples clearly invoke an external CLI. No credential theft or malicious destination was found, so this is a contextual operational risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby backtick execution findings are caused by markdown code fences, inline command names, and command examples. I did not find evidence of Ruby code execution in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is markdown and the backticks delimit examples or inline text. There is no Ruby source file or executable Ruby expression in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Checksum And System Detection Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto and system-reconnaissance findings refer to descriptive metadata, example text, duration guidance, or an install note. SHA-256 checksum verification is not a weak cryptographic algorithm in this context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear in explanatory installer documentation. I did not find code that performs reconnaissance or uses weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Vendor URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to inference.sh installer and checksum endpoints. These URLs are expected for an inference.sh workflow, but they require users to trust that vendor infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and tied to the documented service. They are not evidence of malicious exfiltration, but they are a dependency risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The command uses curl output as shell input. This pattern can execute altered or compromised remote content on the user's machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky pattern is directly present in the quick start command. It is a common supply-chain hazard even when the service itself is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide relies on infsh and npx commands. These are legitimate examples, but they execute local commands and may access network services or local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and user-initiated examples. No command injection vector or hostile payload was found.

content.value_statement: Turning a still image into controlled video motion is hard without model and prompt guidance. This skill helps choose i2v models, write motion prompts, and plan short video workflows.
actual_capabilities: Compares image-to-video models for realistic, stylized, fabric, and general animation tasks.
Provides prompt patterns for camera movement, subject motion, atmosphere, and pacing.
Shows command examples for generating images, animating stills, upscaling, adding audio, and merging media.
Recommends short clip durations and stitching strategies for longer outputs.
Explains common mistakes that cause artifacts, warping, and unpredictable motion.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and access to the listed external model apps.
The guide does not generate videos by itself; it provides workflow and prompt instructions.
Video quality depends on the selected model, input image quality, and service availability.
The quick start includes a remote shell installer pattern that users should review before running.

use_cases.title: Create Social Media Motion Clips
use_cases.description: Turn a finished still image into a short video with subtle camera movement and atmospheric effects.

use_cases.title: Animate Product Visuals
use_cases.description: Choose a model and prompt style for product rotations, moving reflections, fabric flow, or premium reveal shots.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI Video Workflows
use_cases.description: Plan a complete image, animation, upscale, audio, and merge workflow using inference.sh app commands.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Still Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Animate this image into a short video. Recommend the best model, write a subtle motion prompt, and explain the camera movement.

prompt_templates.title: Model Selection Help
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose the right model before generating
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a [portrait, product, landscape, fabric, or illustration] image. Compare the best image-to-video models for it and give one recommended prompt.

prompt_templates.title: Cinemagraph Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a cinemagraph workflow for this still image. Keep only one element moving and suggest duration, motion wording, and loop strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Full Video Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a complete image-to-video pipeline for [goal]. Include source image generation, animation model choice, prompt, upscale step, audio idea, and final merge plan.

output_examples.input: A product photo of a jacket on a plain background.
output_examples.output: Recommended model: Fabric 1.0 for cloth movement. Motion direction: slow orbit, gentle sleeve movement, subtle fabric ripples, and controlled studio lighting shifts.

output_examples.input: A landscape image with mountains, water, and clouds.
output_examples.output: Recommended model: Wan 2.5 i2v for realistic natural motion. Prompt focus: slow pan, water ripples, drifting clouds, mild light changes, and peaceful pacing.

output_examples.input: A portrait that should feel alive but stay realistic.
output_examples.output: Recommended model: Wan 2.5 i2v. Use subtle breathing, one natural blink, slight hair movement, and no dramatic head turn.

best_practices: Use subtle motion and one clear camera direction for each clip.
Start with a high-resolution source image before animation.
Generate short clips first, then stitch clips when longer duration is needed.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for complex action scenes with many competing movements.
Do not use long single generations when short clips would preserve quality.
Do not run remote installer commands before reviewing the source and checksum process.

faq.question: Does this skill create videos directly?
faq.answer: No. It guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through image-to-video model selection, prompting, and inference.sh command workflows.

faq.question: Which model should I use for realistic motion?
faq.answer: The guide recommends Wan 2.5 i2v for realistic motion, portraits, landscapes, and natural movement.

faq.question: Which model is best for fabric or cloth?
faq.answer: The guide recommends Fabric 1.0 for cloth, flags, curtains, liquid-like flow, and material movement.

faq.question: How long should image-to-video clips be?
faq.answer: The guide favors short clips, usually two to five seconds, because longer generations can reduce quality.

faq.question: Can I make a longer video?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide suggests generating multiple short clips with progressive motion and stitching them together.

faq.question: Are there security concerns?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow uses external CLI commands and a remote installer example, so users should review commands before running them.
